title: Quisqualis littorea
intro: Quisqualis littorea, also known as the Rangoon creeper, is a tropical flowering vine native to the Indian Ocean region. The plant has been traditionally used in Ayurvedic medicine to treat various ailments, including fever, diarrhea, and respiratory issues, due to its potential health benefits such as anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties. Bioactive constituents of Quisqualis littorea include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, which are believed to contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional Indian medicine, Quisqualis littorea is often used to make herbal preparations such as decoctions, infusions, and powders, which are used to treat a range of health conditions.
taxonomy: Quisqualis littorea is classified in the kingdom Plantae, specifically within the family Combretaceae. This plant belongs to the order Myrtales and the family Combretaceae. The genus Quisqualis is a part of this classification, with Quisqualis littorea being one of its species. The classification is further broken down into the domain Eukarya and the kingdom Plantae, which is a part of the supergroup Archaeplastida. Quisqualis littorea is also sometimes referred to as Combretum littoreum in some classifications.

Its leaves and roots are believed to possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which can help soothe sore throats and alleviate symptoms of colds and flu. Additionally, Quisqualis littorea is sometimes used to treat skin conditions like eczema and acne due to its antiseptic and antioxidant properties.
